sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 48-year-old female, presents with a history of a recent myocardial infarction for which she underwent a cardiac catheterization and stent placement in the right coronary artery 2 weeks ago.
She reports feeling thankful to be alive but struggling with the aftermath of the event.
She has been taking it easy and doing light work, taking her medications as prescribed.
She denies chest pain since the stent placement but reports occasional shortness of breath when walking up and down stairs.
She has been taking brilinta 90mg twice a day and baby aspirin at night.
She has eaten pizza a few times a week but is trying to watch her salt intake and diet.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of a recent myocardial infarction and stent placement in the right coronary artery.
Review of systems: The patient reports occasional shortness of breath when walking up and down stairs.
Current medications:
Brilinta 90mg twice a day
Baby aspirin once a day
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 127/60.
Physical exam: The patient's blood pressure is within normal limits.
The neck exam is unremarkable, with no jugular venous distention or carotid bruits.
The heart exam reveals a slight 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The lung exam is clear to auscultation bilaterally.
The musculoskeletal exam reveals a clean, dry, and intact right radial artery cardiac cath site with a palpable radial pulse.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:

Assessment: The patient is a 48-year-old female with a history of a recent myocardial infarction and stent placement in the right coronary artery, with a slight systolic ejection murmur and mild mitral regurgitation.
Problem list: Myocardial infarction; Mitral regurgitation; Hypertension (status: unknown); Hyperlipidemia (status: unknown); Cardiac catheterization site complication (status: unknown).
Plan:
I continued the patient on aspirin once a day and brilinta 90mg twice a day for at least one year.
I prescribed crestor 40mg once a day.
I ordered an echocardiogram in six months.
I enrolled the patient in cardiac rehab for dietary education and counseling.
I ordered liver function studies and a lipid panel to follow up in six weeks.
I advised the patient to resume swimming with caution and to take toprol 50mg once a day.
Follow-up: I want to see the patient again in six weeks to follow up on the echocardiogram and lab results.
